segments.title: Master James's Savoyard building world
segments.summary: The opening places Master James of St George in a Savoyard architectural world that shaped major western European castles before his Welsh work.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke frames James as an engineer whose influence is easier to see in castles than in surviving personal records. He places James in the Savoy area spanning modern Switzerland, northern Italy, and southern France, where members of his professional and family circle worked on cathedrals and castles. Lausanne's cathedral and the wider building network show the architectural environment from which James emerged. The section therefore introduces him through the works and workshops around him while retaining caution about details medieval records do not settle.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces James, his broad castle influence, and the Savoy/Lausanne family setting.

segments.title: What the sources cannot prove about James
segments.summary: A source-focused section explains why James's father, apprenticeship, wife, and even translated names remain hard to pin down.
segments.body: This watch point explains the limits behind confident castle-history narratives. Dr. Clarke says Jean or John Cotereel may have been James's father, his apprenticeship master, or one of two related men whom later accounts have combined. He also warns that translated specialist works can blur names and relationships. The absence of a pension for James's wife after his death supplies another inference rather than a firm biographical fact. The caveat matters because the castles are unusually visible while the builder's life must be reconstructed from scattered records.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses uncertainty over whether John or Jean Cotereel was James's father or master.
The transcript covers missing family evidence, pension inference, and the possibility that the named figures may be separate relatives.

segments.title: Vitruvius and master-mason expertise
segments.summary: James's reputation rests on a combination of learned architectural principles and practical command of medieval masonry.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke uses James's reputation as one of medieval Europe's greatest architects to ask what expertise a royal castle builder needed. He singles out familiarity with Vitruvius, the surviving Roman books on architecture, alongside James's practical standing as a master mason. Learned design principles and workshop experience therefore appear as mutually reinforcing skills. That combination helps explain why a king planning unusually demanding fortifications would regard James's technical competence as a strategic asset.

evidence.note: The transcript describes James as a leading medieval architect and connects his skill to Vitruvius and master-mason knowledge.

segments.title: Edward I spots a Savoy castle builder
segments.summary: Edward I's contact with Savoy construction work explains how James moved from continental castle projects toward Wales.
segments.body: The lecture follows Edward's return from crusade in 1273 and his dynastic meeting with Philip I, Count of Savoy. Philip had several castles under construction, with James already running or heavily involved in the work. Edward could therefore judge the builder through visible engineering performance. The contact did not produce an immediate move because Edward had no Welsh castle program underway, but James appears in Wales in 1278 as a works coordinator. The sequence shows recruitment through family politics, patronage, and demonstrated technical ability rather than a modern hiring process.

evidence.note: The transcript says James had built castles before Wales and was involved while Philip of Savoy was constructing several castles.
The transcript describes Edward recognizing good work and James later appearing in Wales in 1278 as a building coordinator.

segments.title: Master of construction in Wales
segments.summary: James's promotion gave him direct royal authority over the great Welsh castle works at Conwy, Caernarfon, Harlech, and Beaumaris.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke highlights the administrative step that turned James from a skilled builder into a powerful royal officer. Edward, frustrated with the pace of construction, made him master of the king's works in Wales. A wage of three shillings a day and direct control over Conwy, Caernarfon, Harlech, and eventually Beaumaris signal the scale of that authority. French and Latin versions of James's title and name also explain why the same official can appear differently across documents. The appointment helps explain why the Ring of Iron functioned as an integrated royal building program instead of a set of isolated local projects.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Edward's impatience with construction speed and James becoming master of construction in Wales.
The transcript lists his full control over Conwy, Caernarfon, Harlech, and Beaumaris and notes name variants in French and Latin.

segments.title: Castle builder as royal military officer
segments.summary: James was trusted not only to design castles but also to hold Harlech and support Edward's Scottish campaigns.
segments.body: This section shows why James should be read as more than a technical specialist. He served as constable of Harlech from 1290 to 1293, making him the king's representative inside a fortress he had helped shape. The office implied trust in his ability to manage and, if necessary, fight the castle. Edward summoned him to Scotland in 1298, and the record places him at Stirling during the 1304 siege. James's career therefore joins design, garrison responsibility, and campaign fortification as different expressions of operational state power.

evidence.note: The transcript says James was appointed constable of Harlech and was considered capable of fighting his castles.
The transcript says James worked at Stirling during the 1304 siege and was called to help Edward in Scotland.

segments.title: Sea-supplied castles as occupation deterrence
segments.summary: The central sea-power argument ties James's castle design to water access, reinforcement, and the deterrent machinery of occupation.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke explains why James belongs in a sea-power account of the Ring of Iron. Edward's castles depended on water access for security, supply, and the arrival of reinforcements. A weak fortress might fall before that maritime lifeline could have any effect, so walls, siting, and terrain use were part of the same logistical system. The castles were therefore engines of occupation and deterrence, not monuments detached from operations. This watch point connects fortress architecture directly to naval logistics and political control.

evidence.note: The transcript calls the castles engines of deterrence and occupation dependent on water access for security and supply.
The transcript says Edward needed a master architect to make castles as strong as possible and suited to the ground.

segments.title: Reading castle construction at Beaumaris
segments.summary: A brief visitor-oriented aside uses Beaumaris to connect idealized castle imagery with the tools and building methods behind the masonry.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke turns Beaumaris Castle into a guide to reading construction evidence on site. He warns that the attractive image shown on screen is highly idealized, then points viewers toward displays inside the castle about tools and building methods. That contrast separates a picturesque finished fortress from the labor and technique that produced it. Opening the video here is useful for learners who want to connect James's reputation with the material practice of medieval castle building.

evidence.note: The transcript distinguishes an idealized Beaumaris image from the castle's displays about construction tools and methods.

segments.title: Which Ring of Iron castles James controlled
segments.summary: A castle-by-castle survey separates sites James clearly controlled from places where his involvement remains likely, limited, or uncertain.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke maps James's responsibility across the Ring of Iron rather than crediting every Welsh castle to one famous architect. Flint, Rhuddlan, Hawarden, Harlech, Caernarfon, Conwy, and Beaumaris form the core of the work attributed to him. Denbigh may reflect his influence because it lay within his operating area, while Aberystwyth and Builth receive more cautious treatment. Good deputies extended his reach, but even a powerful master of works could not supervise every site directly. The survey therefore distinguishes system-wide architectural influence from documented personal control.

evidence.note: The transcript reviews which castles James controlled or influenced and notes limits on what one person could oversee.

segments.title: Flint, Rhuddlan, and Hawarden built for speed
segments.summary: The fast construction of James's first three Welsh responsibilities helps explain why Edward entrusted him with a wider castle program.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke identifies Flint as James's primary early responsibility while also placing him over work at Rhuddlan and Hawarden. These castles belonged to the first group completed and secured quickly. James continued to move among the sites rather than treating each commission as an isolated project. Speed mattered to Edward I, so reliable delivery offered a practical reason to expand James's authority. The episode closes by connecting construction performance with royal confidence and promotion.

evidence.note: The transcript returns to Flint, Rhuddlan, and Hawarden as early fast-built castles and links that speed to Edward's preference.
